content:
Hi all.
There's very little documentation on the state of rust for linux device driver development. I looked at https://rust-for-linux.com/ but there's barely any information. What I want to know is how difficult is it to build a kernel module using rust? I have a Yocto build with kernel 6.1 and building a rust user application is trivial but I want to make a kernel module which makes use of GPIO (simple read write) and PCIe (DMA, register, reset etc). What I was hoping was that I could use something like bindgen to link with the existing C kernel headers and use that in my rust kernel module. From a quick look at this project, it seems that instead I need to build a very customised version of the Kernel with rust kernel headers instead? Can you please give me some indication on the level of effort required to get a rust kernel module up and running? Thanks

Akbar Ramzan said:

Hi all.
There's very little documentation on the state of rust for linux device driver development. I looked at https://rust-for-linux.com/ but there's barely any information. What I want to know is how difficult is it to build a kernel module using rust? I have a Yocto build with kernel 6.1 and building a rust user application is trivial but I want to make a kernel module which makes use of GPIO (simple read write) and PCIe (DMA, register, reset etc). What I was hoping was that I could use something like bindgen to link with the existing C kernel headers and use that in my rust kernel module. From a quick look at this project, it seems that instead I need to build a very customised version of the Kernel with rust kernel headers instead? Can you please give me some indication on the level of effort required to get a rust kernel module up and running? Thanks

You probably want 6.6 because quite a few Rust patches were merged since 6.1. Regarding level of effort, you can take a look at the Rust null block driver for a very simple example and for a more comprehensive example utilizing dma, pci, irq you can look at the nvme driver

it seems that instead I need to build a very customised version of the Kernel with rust kernel headers instead?

What do you mean by "very customized" and "rust kernel headers"? Are you perhaps thinking of out-of-tree modules and trying to use your existing distro kernel?
Yes that's right. We have a well-tested kernel provided by our vendor so we do not wish to make many modifications there and wanted to know how difficult it would be to create an out-of-tree module
You could always backport what you need, but it is probably easier to track latest release for this kind of work. It is very new and patches are added in each release.
Most of the patches for the NVMe driver are not merged upstream and are still sort of "work in progress"
Ok that's fine thanks. I think rust might be too bleeding edge for our use-case and we should consider it for the future
Akbar, the Rust infrastructure in kernel is under very active development and it is not a production-ready yet. I think we might need to wait until 7.0 comes out. That being my very personal opinion.
